concerns.text: The plan relies on a 'Synthetic Corrosion Dataset' for development and CI because no verified real dataset exists. Synthetic data cannot validate the core research hypothesis (associations between real elemental composition and real degradation pathways). Training on synthetic data and claiming 'associational' findings on real data is methodologically invalid; the model will learn the synthetic generator's bias, not material science reality. The plan must secure a real dataset or explicitly reframe the project as a 'synthetic data generator validation' rather than a material degradation predictor.

The plan proposes an 'Out-of-Distribution (OOD) test set' based on held-out alloy classes (e.g., high-entropy alloys) to validate generalization. However, with a target of only ≥200 records total, splitting data to hold out entire alloy classes (which may be rare) will likely result in a test set too small to yield statistically significant macro-F1 scores or reliable permutation test p-values. The sample size is insufficient for the proposed OOD validation strategy.

Success Criterion SC-003 requires validating feature importance against a 'Reference Importance Vector' derived from 5 review papers using Spearman rank correlation (ρ ≥ 0.6). The plan does not specify how to construct this vector from qualitative literature (e.g., 'Cr is important for pitting'). Converting qualitative literature findings into a quantitative rank vector for correlation is methodologically ambiguous and prone to researcher bias. The method for constructing the ground-truth vector is undefined.
